placeholderHeading: His story is being written — with care.
placeholderBody: The founder of this campaign is 13 years old. He is still in school. He is still going through it. We are not publishing his story until his guardian, a child psychologist, and our legal reviewer have all signed off. When it's ready, you'll find it here.

story.title: I am 13. I have been bullied for years. This is why I started this.
story.body:
They had a game they played at school. They called it the Invisible Boy.

The rules were simple. I was invisible — so everyone could hit me. Because if someone is invisible, it doesn't count, right?

It counted.

Every day, walking to school, I braced myself. Pushed in the corridor. Prodded on the stairs. Shoved from behind when I wasn't looking. It became routine — the kind of routine that makes your stomach drop before you even reach the gate.

Things disappeared from my pencil box constantly. New pens, new pencils — gone the same day I brought them. I stopped telling my parents I needed new ones because I knew they'd just vanish again.

One day I found a notebook. Someone had written in it — hundreds of ways to bully me. Page after page. They had put thought into it. They had made it a project.

So many mornings I didn't want to go to school. I would cry — really cry, the kind where your whole chest hurts — in front of my parents, in front of my teachers. I told them everything.

They called me horizontally challenged. They thought that was funny.

You have no idea how it feels when you are eating lunch and someone asks you to leave the table with your food — just because you are not their type.

The worst part wasn't any single thing they did. The worst part was how unhappy I felt — all the time. That heaviness that sits on you when you wake up and remember you have to go back.

But my parents taught me something: don't stay quiet. Never stay quiet.

I have raised alarms. I have told every adult I trust. And some things got a little better, for a while. But the system doesn't change. There is no law that says a school must protect me. No law that says there are consequences when they don't. Just guidelines that no one enforces.

So now I am taking things into my own hands.

I am 13 years old. I cannot vote. I cannot file a bill. I cannot walk into Parliament.

But I can collect 100,000 signatures from people who believe that no kid should have to go through what I went through. And I can hand those signatures to the people who can change the law.

I need your help. One signature. That is all it takes to be one more voice that Parliament cannot ignore.
